topic.name: Historical and Cultural Practices
topic.description: The documents explore various historical and cultural practices across different civilizations, focusing on execution methods, religious artifacts, societal structures, and folk traditions.

Document 1: Impalement, as a method of execution, is the penetration of a human by an object such as a stake, pole, spear, or hook.
It was used particularly in response to 'crimes against the state' and regarded across a number of cultures as a very harsh form of capital punishment.
Document 2: Kaumodaki is the gada (mace) of the Hindu god Vishnu.
The name 'Kaumodaki' first appears in the Hindu epic Mahabharata.
Document 3: The Han dynasty was an age of great economic, technological, cultural, and social progress in China.
Its laws, customs, literature, and education were largely guided by the philosophy and ethical system of Confucianism.
Document 4: Munneswaram temple is an important regional Hindu temple complex in Sri Lanka.
It has been in existence at least since 1000 CE, although myths surrounding the temple associate it with the popular Indian epic Ramayana.
Document 5: The Mari Lwyd is a wassailing folk custom found in South Wales.
The tradition entails the use of an eponymous hobby horse which is made from a horse's skull mounted on a pole.
Document 6: Al-Aqsa Mosque is the third holiest site in Islam and is located in the Old City of Jerusalem.
Muslims believe that Muhammad was transported from the Sacred Mosque in Mecca to al-Aqsa during the Night Journey.
Document 7: The Sahifah of al-Ridha is a collection of 240 hadiths attributed to Ali ibn Musa al-Ridha, the eighth Shiite Imam.
It contains hadiths on various topics including the invocation of Allah and the importance of praying five times a day.

topic.name: Historical Figures and Events
topic.description: This cluster discusses notable historical figures and events that shaped various industries and societal changes.

Document 1: Henri L 'Estrange, known as the Australian Blondin, was an Australian successful funambulist and accident prone aeronautical balloonist.

Document 2: The 2009 Royal Mail industrial disputes is an industrial dispute in the United Kingdom involving Royal Mail and members of the Communication Workers Union.
It was the country 's first industrial action involving postal workers since 2007.
Document 3: Avondale Agriculture Research Station or Avondale Discovery Farm is one of thirteen research farms and stations operated by Western Australia 's Department of Agriculture and Food.
Avondale has historical buildings, a farming equipment museum and operates as an agriculture education centre.
Document 4: The Inland Customs Line was a customs barrier built by the British across India primarily to collect the salt tax.
The customs line was begun while India was under the control of the East India Company but continued into the period of direct British rule.
Document 5: Bouldering is a form of rock climbing that is performed without the use of ropes or harnesses.
The sport originated as a method of training for roped climbs and mountaineering.
Document 6: Kiliaen van Rensselaer was a Dutch diamond and pearl merchant from Amsterdam who was one of the founders and directors of the Dutch West India Company.
He was likely the leading proponent of the Charter of Freedoms and Exemptions, the document that established the patroon system.
Document 7: Charles Melville Hays was the president of the Grand Trunk Railway.
He is credited with the formation of the Grand Trunk Pacific Railway, a dream he had to create a second transcontinental railroad within the borders of Canada.

topic.name: American Historical Figures
topic.description: This topic encompasses notable individuals in American history who played significant roles in military, political, and social developments from the colonial period through the Civil War era.

Document 1: Lawrence Berry Washington was an American lawyer, military officer, author, and a member of the Washington family.
Washington served as a second lieutenant in the Virginia Volunteers during the Mexican – American War.
Document 2: Major-General Humphrey Atherton held the highest military rank in colonial New England.
He was active in the governance of the colony, taking part in the acquisition of Native American lands.
Document 3: The Great Dismal Swamp maroons were freed and escaped slaves who inhabited the marshlands.
Research suggests that thousands lived there between about 1700 and the 1860s.
Document 5: Isaac Parsons was an American planter, politician, and militia officer in Virginia.
Parsons served as a member of the Virginia House of Delegates representing Hampshire County.
Document 6: William Stephen Hamilton was a politician and miner who lived much of his life in Illinois and territorial Wisconsin.
Hamilton served in various political offices and as a commander in two Midwest Indian Wars.
Document 7: Benedict Arnold was a general during the American Revolutionary War who originally fought for the American Continental Army.
Arnold planned to surrender Fort West Point to the British forces.

topic.name: Military Aviation History
topic.description: This topic encompasses the history and evolution of military aviation units and operations across various countries during significant conflicts, particularly World War II.

Document 1: The United States Air Force's 102d Intelligence Wing is a military intelligence unit located at Otis Air National Guard Base, Massachusetts.
The history of the 102d begins with the 318th Fighter Group, which was active during World War II.
Document 2: The history of the Swiss Air Force began in 1914 with the establishment of an ad hoc force.
The Swiss Air Force as an autonomous military service was created in October 1936.
Document 3: Air vice-marshal James Edgar Johnson was a Royal Air Force (RAF) pilot and flying ace who flew and fought during the Second World War.
Johnson was involved in heavy aerial fighting during this period.
Document 4: Egon Mayer was a German Luftwaffe military aviator during World War II, a fighter ace credited with 102 enemy aircraft shot down.
Mayer fought in the Battle of France and claimed his first aerial victory in that campaign.
Document 5: No. 79 Wing was a Royal Australian Air Force (RAAF) wing of World War II.
It took part in the New Guinea and North-Western Area Campaigns during 1944 – 45.
Document 6: Northern Area Command was one of several geographically based commands raised by the Royal Australian Air Force (RAAF) during World War II.
It was primarily responsible for air defence, aerial reconnaissance and protection of the sea lanes.
Document 7: No. 3 Elementary Flying Training School was a Royal Australian Air Force (RAAF) pilot training unit that operated during World War II.
It provided introductory flight instruction to new pilots as part of Australia's contribution to the Empire Air Training Scheme.

topic.name: Naval Warships of the 19th and 20th Centuries
topic.description: This topic covers the design, history, and operational roles of various naval warships built for different navies during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

Document 1: HMS Cyclops was the lead ship of the Cyclops-class breastwork monitors built for the Royal Navy during the 1870s.
The ship spent most of her career in reserve; her only sustained period in commission was four months in Portland Harbour.
Document 2: HMS Havelock was an H-class destroyer that had originally been ordered by the Brazilian Navy.
She participated in the Norwegian Campaign in May 1940 and was assigned to convoy escort and anti-submarine patrols.
Document 3: Ekaterina II was the lead ship of the Ekaterina II-class pre-dreadnought battleships built for the Imperial Russian Navy in the 1880s.
She was turned over to the Sevastopol port authorities before being stricken on 14 August 1907.
Document 4: Yashima was a Fuji-class pre-dreadnought battleship built for the Imperial Japanese Navy in the 1890s.
She participated in the early stages of the Russo-Japanese War of 1904 – 1905.
Document 5: The Admiral Hipper class was a group of five heavy cruisers built by Nazi Germany's Kriegsmarine in the mid-1930s.
Only the first three ships of the class saw action during World War II.
Document 6: HMS Hasty was an H-class destroyer built for the Royal Navy during the mid-1930s.
She was assigned to the Mediterranean Fleet until the beginning of World War II.
Document 7: HMS Exmouth was an E-class destroyer flotilla leader built for the Royal Navy in the early 1930s.
She was sunk by a German submarine in January 1940 while escorting a merchant ship north of Scotland.

topic.name: Historical Architecture and Cultural Heritage
topic.description: This topic encompasses significant historical buildings and architectural styles that reflect cultural heritage across different regions and time periods.

Document 1: The Ganting Grand Mosque is a Cultural Property of Indonesia.
It is one of the oldest in Indonesia.
Document 2: Architecture in early modern Scotland encompasses all building within the borders of the kingdom of Scotland.
The impact of the Renaissance on Scottish architecture began in the reign of James III.
Document 3: Bath Abbey is one of the largest examples of Perpendicular Gothic architecture in the West Country.
It is an active place of worship and a heritage museum.
Document 4: Hampton National Historic Site preserves a remnant of a vast 18th-century estate.
It is considered to be one of the finest examples of Georgian architecture in the U.S.
Document 5: Beeston Castle is a former Royal castle built in the 1220s.
The castle is now in ruins and is a Scheduled Ancient Monument.
Document 6: Harmondsworth Great Barn is the largest timber-framed building in England.

Document 7: Castell y Bere was constructed by Llywelyn the Great in the 1220s.

topic.name: Military Engagements in History
topic.description: The documents discuss various military battles and sieges that occurred during different historical periods and conflicts.

Document 1: The Battle of Iron Works Hill, also known as the Battle of Mount Holly, was a series of minor skirmishes that took place on December 22 and 23, 1776, during the American War of Independence.

the action prevented von Donop from being in his assigned base at Bordentown, New Jersey and in a position to assist Johann Rall's brigade in Trenton, New Jersey when it was attacked and defeated by George Washington.
Document 2: The [First] Battle of Stockach occurred on 25 March 1799, when French and Austrian armies fought for control of the geographically strategic Hegau region in present-day Baden-Württemberg.
the French were driven back upon the Rhine River.
Document 4: A Siege of Tyana was carried out by the Umayyad Caliphate in 707 – 708 / 708 – 709 in retaliation for a heavy defeat of an Umayyad army under Maimun the Mardaite by the Byzantine Empire in c. 706.
the city was plundered and largely destroyed, and according to Byzantine sources its people were made captive and deported, leaving the city deserted.
Document 5: The Battle of Noordhorn, fought on 30 September 1581, was a pitched battle of the Dutch Revolt, fought between a Spanish army commanded by Colonel Francisco Verdugo and a Dutch States rebel army under the Englishman John Norreys.
the battle was fought on a rough, marshy ground very favourable to the Spanish army.
Document 6: The Siege of Fort Stanwix began on August 2, 1777, and ended August 22.
the siege was finally broken when American reinforcements under the command of Benedict Arnold neared.
Document 7: The Campaign of Grodno was a plan developed by Johann Patkul and Otto Arnold von Paykull during the Swedish invasion of the Polish – Lithuanian Commonwealth, a part of the Great Northern War.
the campaign led to Sweden gaining control over the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, until the Swedish defeat at Battle of Poltava.

topic.name: Military Aircraft Development
topic.description: This topic encompasses the design, development, and operational history of various military aircraft across different eras and nations.

Document 1: The Boeing E-3 Sentry, commonly known as AWACS, is an airborne early warning and control (AEW&C) aircraft developed by Boeing as the prime contractor.
Production ended in 1992 after 68 aircraft had been built.
Document 2: The Vought F6U Pirate was the Vought company's first jet fighter, designed for US Navy during the mid-1940s.
Although pioneering the use of turbojet power as the first naval fighter with an afterburner and composite material construction, the aircraft proved to be underpowered.
Document 3: The North American XB-21, also known by the manufacturer's model designation NA-21, was a prototype bomber aircraft developed by North American Aviation in the late 1930s.
Only the prototype was ever built.
Document 4: Lockheed Have Blue was the code name for Lockheed's demonstrator that preceded the F-117 Nighthawk production stealth aircraft.
The aircraft's plate-like, faceted shape was designed to deflect electromagnetic waves.
Document 5: The Heinkel He 111 was a German aircraft designed by Siegfried and Walter Günter at Heinkel Flugzeugwerke in 1934.
The Heinkel He 111 was the most numerous Luftwaffe bomber during the early stages of World War II.
Document 6: The Avro Canada CF-105 Arrow was a delta-winged interceptor aircraft designed and built by Avro Canada.
The Arrow is considered to have been an advanced technical and aerodynamic achievement for the Canadian aviation industry.
Document 7: The Blackburn Firebrand was a British single-engine strike fighter for the Fleet Air Arm of the Royal Navy designed during World War II.
Development was slow and the first production aircraft was not delivered until after the end of the war.

topic.name: Medieval Rulers and Dynasties
topic.description: This topic encompasses the lives and reigns of significant medieval rulers and their impact on regional politics and society.

Document 1: Tamar reigned as Queen regnant of Georgia from 1184 to 1213
her position as the first woman to rule Georgia in her own right was emphasized by the title mep'e
Document 2: Sejanus was an ambitious soldier, friend and confidant of the Roman Emperor Tiberius
Sejanus rose to power as prefect of the Roman imperial bodyguard
Document 3: Olaf the Black was a mid 13th century sea-king who ruled the Isle of Man
Óláfr was the son of Guðrøðr Óláfsson, King of the Isles
Document 4: Peter of Canterbury was the first abbot of the monastery of SS. Peter and Paul in Canterbury
Peter was a companion of Augustine in the Gregorian mission to Kent
Document 5: Ascall mac Ragnaill was the last Norse-Gaelic King of Dublin
Control of the wealthy coastal kingdom was bitterly contested during Ascall's floruit
Document 6: Béla IV was King of Hungary and Croatia between 1235 and 1270
He attempted to restore royal authority, which had diminished under his father
Document 7: Sitric Cáech was a Viking leader who ruled Dublin and then Viking Northumbria
He successfully recaptured Dublin and established himself as king

topic.name: Fungal Species Descriptions
topic.description: The documents provide detailed taxonomic and ecological information about various species of fungi, including their characteristics, habitats, and historical classifications.

Document 1: Entoloma murrayi, commonly known as the yellow unicorn Entoloma or the unicorn pinkgill, is a species of fungus in the Entolomataceae family.
The fungus produces yellow mushrooms that have a characteristic sharp umbo on the top of a conical cap.
Document 2: Antrodia serialiformis is a species of polypore fungus in the family Fomitopsidaceae.
Differences in ecology, spore size, and DNA sequences distinguish the two lookalikes.
Document 3: Russula densifolia, commonly known as the crowded russula or the reddening russula, is a species of agaric fungus in the family Russulaceae.
The mushrooms are characterized by the red and then black color changes that occur in the flesh when it is bruised.
Document 4: Glutinoglossum glutinosum, commonly known as the viscid black earth tongue or the glutinous earthtongue, is a species of fungus in the family Geoglossaceae.
It has been found in northern Africa, Asia, Europe, and North America.
Document 5: Gyromitra caroliniana, known commonly as the Carolina false morel or big red, is an ascomycete fungus of the genus Gyromitra.
Although it is collected and eaten by some, there is some suggestion it may contain the toxic compound gyromitrin.
Document 6: Mycena purpureofusca, commonly known as the purple edge bonnet, is a species of agaric fungus in the family Mycenaceae.
The fungus contains a laccase enzyme that has been investigated scientifically for its potential to detoxify recalcitrant industrial dyes.
Document 7: Lactarius rufulus, commonly known as the rufous candy cap, is a species of fungus in the Russulaceae family.
The species, known only from California, Arizona, and Mexico, grows on the ground in leaf litter near oak trees.

topic.name: International Sporting Events
topic.description: The documents discuss various international sporting events and competitions involving different countries and athletes.

Document 1: The Junior Eurovision Song Contest is a song competition which has been organised by the European Broadcasting Union (EBU) annually since 2003.
Each participating broadcaster sends an act, the members of which are aged 10 to 15 on the day of the contest.
Document 2: Zersenay Tadese is an Eritrean long-distance track and road running athlete.
He currently holds the men's half marathon world record.
Document 3: Afghanistan participated in the 2002 Asian Games held in Busan, South Korea.
This marked Afghanistan's return to international sporting events after the fall of the Taliban regime.
Document 4: Ulla Salzgeber is a German equestrian and Olympic champion who competes in the sport of dressage.
Competing in the 2000 and 2004 Summer Olympics, she won two team gold medals.
Document 5: The Cayman Islands sent a delegation to compete at the 2008 Summer Olympics in Beijing, China.
The delegation included fifteen people; of the delegation, four athletes participated under the Caymanian flag.
Document 6: Mauritania competed at the 2004 Summer Olympics in Athens, Greece.
The country's participation at Athens marked its sixth appearance in the Summer Olympics since its debut in the 1984 Summer Olympics.
Document 7: The ABU Radio Song Festival 2012 was the first edition of the biennial ABU Radio Song Festivals.
The festival took place in the KBS Hall, which is located in the South Korean capital of Seoul.

topic.name: Bach's Church Cantatas and Sacred Music
topic.description: The documents discuss various church cantatas and sacred music composed by Johann Sebastian Bach, highlighting their historical context, musical structure, and theological themes.

Document 1: Bach wrote the cantata to conclude his first Christmas season as Thomaskantor in Leipzig
The text by an anonymous author combines the prescribed readings for the feast day
Document 2: Bach wrote the cantata in his second year in Leipzig for the Sunday Exaudi
The prescribed readings for the feast day were from the First Epistle of Peter
Document 4: The Mass in B minor is Johann Sebastian Bach's only setting of the complete Latin text of the Ordinarium missae
Bach structured the work in four parts
Document 5: Bach composed it in Leipzig for Cantate Sunday
The topic is the prediction of Jesus of his parting and the coming of the Spirit as a comforter
Document 6: Bach composed the chorale cantata in Leipzig for the Second Sunday after Epiphany
It is a meditation on Jesus as a comforter in distress
Document 7: Johann Sebastian Bach's Magnificat is a musical setting of the biblical canticle Magnificat
It is the first major liturgical composition on a Latin text by Bach

topic.name: Film and Cinema History
topic.description: The documents discuss various aspects of film history, including notable films, actors, directors, and the evolution of cinema across different cultures.

1: Ouw Peh Tjoa is a 1934 film from the Dutch East Indies.
It was directed and produced by The Teng Chun.
2: Winona Ryder is an American actress known for her roles in iconic films of the 1990s.
She won a Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actress.
3: Jaws 3-D is a 1983 American thriller film and the third installment in the Jaws franchise.
The film is notable for making use of 3D film during the revived interest in the technology in the 1980s.
4: The Girl is a 2012 British television film about Alfred Hitchcock and his leading ladies.
It depicts Hitchcock's obsession with actress Tippi Hedren.
5: Devika Rani was an actress in Indian films who was active during the 1930s and 1940s.
She is widely acknowledged as the first lady of Indian cinema.
6: Paresh Mokashi is an Indian filmmaker known for his work in Marathi cinema.
He directed the acclaimed film Harishchandrachi Factory, depicting the making of India's first full-length feature film.
7: Savanna Samson is a former pornographic actress known for her roles in adult films.
She has gained mainstream recognition with appearances on television shows.

topic.name: Interfaith Dialogue and Ethics
topic.description: This topic encompasses discussions on the intersections of various religious beliefs, ethical considerations, and the implications of moral arguments within diverse spiritual contexts.

Document 1: Lynn de Silva is widely regarded as one of the foremost Christian practitioners of Buddhist-Christian dialogue in Sri Lanka
Possibly his most notable contribution to theology is the book titled The Problem of the Self in Buddhism and Christianity.
Document 4: The argument from morality is an argument for the existence of God
Arguments from morality tend to be based on moral normativity or moral order.

topic.name: Contributions to Science and Society
topic.description: The documents highlight the significant contributions of various individuals to science, education, and cultural movements, emphasizing their roles in advancing knowledge and societal progress.

Document 1: instrumental in putting the university on a solid legal and financial footing
His monetary gifts to his alma mater made possible the construction of two student residences
Document 2: served at the Manhattan Project's Metallurgical Laboratory
became the head of J (Test) Division at Los Alamos
Document 3: pioneer of the use of radioactive isotopes in biology and medicine
published a biography of George de Hevesy
Document 4: prominent advocate of the religion of Thelema
played a key role in propagating Thelema across the continent
Document 5: first woman to have trained a winner of that race
accomplishments came against the backdrop of having been diagnosed with cardiomyopathy
Document 6: one of the last surviving people to have worked on the Manhattan Project
credited with the insight that a solid sub-critical mass of plutonium could be explosively compressed
Document 7: received national media attention in 1982 when he was expelled from Argentina
wrote the book Mektig og avmektig which received good reviews

topic.name: Mathematical and Engineering Concepts
topic.description: This cluster discusses various mathematical theories and engineering principles, particularly in the fields of electrical engineering and topology.

1: Islamic geometric patterns tend to avoid using figurative images, makes frequent use of geometric patterns which have developed over the centuries.
Geometric patterns occur in a variety of forms in Islamic art and architecture including kilim carpets, Persian girih and Moroccan zellige tilework.
2: A composite image filter is an electronic filter consisting of multiple image filter sections of two or more different types.
Filters designed by this method are called image parameter filters, or just image filters.
3: The mobility analogy is a method of representing a mechanical system by an analogous electrical system.
The mathematical behaviour of the simulated electrical system is identical to the mathematical behaviour of the represented mechanical system.
4: Foster's reactance theorem is an important theorem in the fields of electrical network analysis and synthesis.
The theorem states that the reactance of a passive, lossless two-terminal network always strictly monotonically increases with frequency.
5: A waffle-iron filter is a type of waveguide filter used at microwave frequencies for signal filtering.
Design techniques for waffle-iron filters include image-parameter methods, network synthesis methods and numerical analysis methods.
6: The Mayer – Vietoris sequence is an algebraic tool to help compute algebraic invariants of topological spaces, known as their homology and cohomology groups.
The sequence relates the (co) homology groups of the space to the (co) homology groups of the subspaces.
7: Addition is one of the four basic operations of arithmetic, with the others being subtraction, multiplication and division.
In arithmetic, rules for addition involving fractions and negative numbers have been devised amongst others.

topic.name: Naval Engagements of the French Revolutionary Wars
topic.description: The documents discuss various naval battles and actions involving the British Royal Navy and the French Navy during the French Revolutionary Wars.

Document 1: minor naval engagement during the French Revolutionary Wars
British Royal Navy frigates and two frigates and a cutter from the Navy of the Batavian Republic
Document 2: minor naval engagement fought along the Biscay coast of France during the French Revolutionary Wars
Royal Navy's Channel Fleet to institute a close blockade on the French naval ports
Document 3: Vengeur du Peuple was a 74-gun ship of the line of the French Navy
took part in the Glorious First of June
Document 4: Battle of the Raz de Sein was a naval engagement of the blockade of Brest during the French Revolutionary Wars
British blockade fleet under Admiral Lord Bridport
Document 5: inconclusive minor naval engagement between small French Navy and British Royal Navy squadrons
French squadron engaged on a commerce raiding operation
Document 6: minor naval action fought between a British ship of the line and a French frigate
French Navy sought to disrupt British trade
Document 7: Invasion of the Cape Colony was a British military expedition launched in 1795
British operations against the Dutch Empire to prevent the use of its facilities by the French Navy

topic.name: The Boat Race History
topic.description: A historical overview of the annual rowing competition between the University of Oxford and the University of Cambridge on the River Thames.

Document 1: The 28th Boat Race between crews from the University of Oxford and the University of Cambridge took place on the River Thames on the 1 April 1871.
The race was first held in 1829, and since 1845 has taken place on the 4.2-mile (6.8 km) Championship Course on the River Thames in southwest London.
Document 2: The 73rd Boat Race took place on 30 March 1921.
The rivalry is a major point of honour between the two universities and followed throughout the United Kingdom.
Document 5: The 99th Boat Race took place on 28 March 1953.
It is followed throughout the United Kingdom and, as of 2014, broadcast worldwide.
Document 7: The 145th Boat Race took place on 3 April 1999.
The rivalry is a major point of honour between the two universities and followed throughout the United Kingdom.

topic.name: Provincial Highways in Ontario and Michigan
topic.description: This topic encompasses the various provincial and state highways in Ontario, Canada, and Michigan, USA, detailing their routes, historical significance, and regional characteristics.

Document 1: Ontario Highway 3, commonly referred to as Highway 3, is a provincially maintained highway in the Canadian province of Ontario
The total length of Highway 3 is 259.2 km (161.1 mi)
Document 2: Ontario Highway 77, commonly referred to as Highway 77, is a provincially maintained highway in the Canadian province of Ontario
Highway 77 serves to interconnect Highway 3 near Leamington with Highway 401 near Tilbury.
Document 3: M-17 is a 6.390-mile-long (10.284 km) state trunkline highway in the U.S. state of Michigan
M-17 begins at exit 37 along US Highway 23 (US 23) on the Ann Arbor – Pittsfield Township border.
Document 4: M-19 is a state trunkline highway in the US state of Michigan
The highway runs through mostly rural and agricultural areas, connecting several small communities.
Document 6: Highway 407 is a tolled 400-series highway in the Canadian province of Ontario
It begins in Burlington and travels through the Greater Toronto Area (GTA) suburbs.

topic.name: Marine and Avian Species
topic.description: The documents discuss various species of marine animals and birds, highlighting their habitats, behaviors, and conservation statuses.

Document 1: The Atlantic blue marlin (Makaira nigricans) is a species of marlin endemic to the Atlantic Ocean.
Marlin is a popular game fish.
Document 2: The spinner shark (Carcharhinus brevipinna) is a species of requiem shark.
This species occurs in tropical and warm temperate waters worldwide.
Document 3: The Andean cock-of-the-rock (Rupicola peruvianus) is a large passerine bird of the cotinga family native to Andean cloud forests in South America.
It is widely regarded as the national bird of Peru.
Document 4: The whitetip reef shark (Triaenodon obesus) is a species of requiem shark.
It is typically found on or near the bottom in clear water.
Document 5: The New Guinea crocodile (Crocodylus novaeguineae) is a small species of crocodile found on the island of New Guinea.
This crocodile was over-hunted for its valuable skin in the mid 20th century.
Document 6: The Tasmanian numbfish (Narcine tasmaniensis) is a species of electric ray in the family Narcinidae.
Bottom-dwelling and sedentary, the Tasmanian numbfish feeds mainly on polychaete worms and crustaceans.
Document 7: The little owl (Athene noctua) is a bird that inhabits much of the temperate and warmer parts of Europe.
This owl is a member of the typical or true owl family, Strigidae.

topic.name: Biodiversity and Evolutionary Biology
topic.description: This topic encompasses the study of various species across different taxonomic groups, their evolutionary relationships, and adaptations in diverse environments.

1: A catalytic triad refers to the three amino acid residues that function together at the centre of the active site of some hydrolase and transferase enzymes.
Chemical constraints on catalysis have led to the same catalytic solution independently evolving in at least 23 separate superfamilies.
2: The molluscs or mollusks compose the large phylum of invertebrate animals known as the Mollusca.
Molluscs are highly diverse, not just in size and in anatomical structure, but also in behaviour and in habitat.
3: The Chittenango ovate amber snail is a species of small air-breathing land snail in the family Succineidae.
This species was discovered in 1905 and was reported three years later as a subspecies of the oval ambersnail.
4: The Lepidoptera is an order of insects that includes moths and butterflies.
Lepidopteran species are characterized by more than three derived features, some of the most apparent being the scales covering their bodies and wings.
5: Aloe vera is a succulent plant species of the genus Aloe.
Aloe vera leaves contain phytochemicals under study for possible bioactivity.
6: Opabinia regalis is an extinct, stem group arthropod found in the Middle Cambrian Burgess Shale Lagerstätte of British Columbia, Canada.
Opabinia was a soft-bodied animal of modest size, and its segmented body had lobes along the sides and a fan-shaped tail.
7: Novomessor ensifer is a species of ant endemic to Mexico.
N. ensifer was originally a part of the genus Aphaenogaster until a recent phylogenetic study concluded that Novomessor was genetically distinct and should be separated.

topic.name: Military Units in World Wars
topic.description: The documents collectively discuss various military units from the United States and Australia that served during World War I and World War II, detailing their formations, campaigns, and contributions.

Document 1: The 18th Engineer Brigade...served in Europe during World War II
The brigade later deployed to Vietnam, during the Vietnam War
Document 2: The 2 / 6th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army that served during the Second World War
the battalion formed part of the 6th Division and was among the first troops raised by Australia during the war
Document 3: The 24th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the United States Army
Formed during World War II from the disbanding Hawaiian Division
Document 4: Father Francis Gleeson...served as a British Army chaplain during Irelands' involvement in the First World War
Commissioned into the Army Chaplains' Department and attached to the 2nd Battalion, Royal Munster Fusiliers
Document 5: The 29th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army
First formed in 1915 for service during the First World War
Document 6: The 2 / 14th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army that served during World War II
After completing training in Australia in 1940, the battalion deployed to the Middle East
Document 7: The 38th ( Welsh ) Division...was active during both the First and Second World Wars
the division's baptism by fire came during the opening days of the Battle of the Somme

topic.name: Video Game Expansions and Spin-offs
topic.description: This topic encompasses various video games that are expansions or spin-offs of existing franchises, highlighting their development, gameplay elements, and reception.

Document 1: Half-Life : Blue Shift is an expansion pack for Valve Software's science fiction first-person shooter video game Half-Life.
The game was developed by Gearbox Software with Valve Corporation and published by Sierra Entertainment.
Document 3: Angry Birds is a puzzle video game developed by Finnish computer game developer Rovio Mobile that started the Angry Birds franchise.
The series has since expanded to include titles for dedicated video game consoles and for PCs.
Document 4: Pokémon Pinball : Ruby & Sapphire is a pinball game developed by Jupiter and published by Nintendo for the Game Boy Advance handheld game console.
It is based on Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, and is a sequel to Pokémon Pinball for the Game Boy Color.
Document 7: Final Fantasy Adventure, originally released in Japan as Seiken Densetsu : Final Fantasy Gaiden, is a Final Fantasy spinoff and the first game in the Mana series.
A remake, Sword of Mana, was released in 2003 changing the plot and many gameplay aspects.

topic.name: Political Leadership and Elections
topic.description: This topic encompasses various aspects of political leadership, electoral processes, and the impact of political figures in different regions and historical contexts.

1: The 2012 United States Senate election in Massachusetts took place on November 6, 2012, concurrently with the U.S. presidential election.
Warren clinched near-unanimous party support, with all but one of the other Democratic candidates withdrawing following her entrance.
2: The National Front is a socially conservative, nationalist political party in France.
Most political commentators place the FN on the right to far right but party representatives reject this.
3: Andry Rajoelina became president on 21 March 2009 during a political crisis.
He stepped down on 25 January 2014 following internationally recognized general elections held in 2013.
4: James Fisher Robinson was the 22nd Governor of Kentucky, serving the remainder of the unfinished term of Governor Beriah Magoffin.
Robinson opposed both secession and abolition.
5: Bo Xilai is a Chinese former politician who came to prominence through his tenures as the mayor of Dalian and then the governor of Liaoning.
He was found guilty of corruption and sentenced to life imprisonment.
6: David Watts Morgan was a Welsh trade unionist, a Labour politician, and a Member of Parliament from 1918 to 1933.
Morgan encouraged Rhondda miners to enlist in the army in 1914 following the outbreak of the First World War.
7: Toni Preckwinkle is the current Cook County Board President and a former alderman in the Chicago City Council.
She emerged as the council's prominent defender of affordable housing.

topic.name: Pulp and Genre Literature
topic.description: The documents explore various forms of pulp and genre literature, including science fiction, travel writing, comics, and short stories.

Document 1: Dynamic Science Fiction was a pulp magazine which published six issues from December 1952 to January 1954.
It was launched at the end of the pulp era.
Document 2: Thrilling Cities is the title of a travelogue by the James Bond author Ian Fleming.
The book was first published in the UK in November 1963 by Jonathan Cape.
Document 3: Thrud the Barbarian is a comics character created by Carl Critchlow in 1981.
Thrud himself is a parody of Conan the Barbarian.
Document 4: Kawan Bergeloet is a collection of short stories written by Soeman Hs and first published by Balai Pustaka in 1941.
These stories are generally humorous in nature.
Document 5: Attack on Titan is a Japanese manga series written and illustrated by Hajime Isayama.
The series began in Kodansha 's Bessatsu Shōnen Magazine on September 9, 2009.
Document 6: The Witch 's Tales was a pulp magazine edited by Tom Chadburn which published two issues in November and December 1936.
It was a companion to a radio program.
Document 7: Japan Echo was an English-language periodical on Japanese issues which was initially published in print form by Japan Echo Inc. between 1974 and 2010.
Japan Echo was launched with the support of Japan 's Foreign Affairs Ministry.

topic.name: Energy Production Technologies
topic.description: The documents discuss various energy production technologies, including solar power and oil shale extraction, highlighting their significance and applications.

Document 1: solar power plants in the Mojave Desert which supply power to the electricity grid
Insolation (solar radiation) in the Mojave Desert is among the best available in the United States
Document 2: Oil shale (Estonian: põlevkivi) is a strategic energy resource
the oil shale industry in Estonia is one of the most developed in the world

topic.name: Post-Colonial Leadership and Conflict
topic.description: This topic encompasses the political leadership and conflicts that arose in various regions following the end of colonial rule, highlighting the struggles for independence and the subsequent governance challenges.

Document 1: The Congo Crisis was a period of political upheaval and conflict in the Republic of the Congo between 1960 and 1965.
It began almost immediately after the Congo became independent from Belgium.
Document 4: Félix Houphouët-Boigny was the first President of Côte d'Ivoire, serving for more than three decades until his death.
He played a significant role in the decolonization of Africa.

topic.name: The X-Files Episodes
topic.description: The documents discuss various episodes of the American science fiction television series The X-Files, focusing on paranormal investigations by FBI agents.

Document 1: the episode was written by series creator Chris Carter and Frank Spotnitz
the two find Spender to be a surprise ally
Document 2: the episode follows the Fringe team's investigation into the kidnapping of a young musical prodigy
the episode featured her character in a 'pretty violent and quite messy' fight
Document 3: Mulder investigates a small town plagued by deaths in which the bodies are found covered in cockroaches
the episode received mostly positive reviews from critics
Document 4: a little boy mysteriously reappears after having been kidnapped for ten years
the case stirs up painful memories for Doggett
Document 5: Mulder and Scully investigate several killings blamed on an Asian dog
the episode received mostly negative reviews from critics
Document 6: Mulder disobeys orders to stay away from the X-Files
they soon discover that the crew has been infected with the black oil
Document 7: Mulder and Scully investigate a high school student who is the prime suspect in the bizarre murder of a police officer
the idea for 'Rush' had been proposed as far back as the sixth season

topic.name: Tropical Cyclones and Hurricanes
topic.description: This cluster discusses various tropical cyclones and hurricanes, detailing their formation, intensity, and impacts across different regions and years.

Document 1: Hurricane Cosme was an unusually large tropical cyclone that made landfall in south-western Mexico in June 1989.
It caused excessive rainfall on land, leading to deadly and destructive flooding.
Document 2: The 1945 Texas hurricane was a slow-moving tropical cyclone which paralleled the Texas Gulf Coast, causing extensive damage.
The hurricane quickly slowed in forward motion, allowing it time to intensify off the Texas coast.
Document 3: Tropical Storm Jerry was a short-lived tropical storm that formed in the latter half of the 2001 Atlantic hurricane season.
Jerry caused minimal effects in the Lesser Antilles.
Document 4: The 2002 – 03 Australian region cyclone season included Cyclone Inigo, which tied Cyclone Gwenda in 1999 as the most intense tropical cyclone on record in the Australian basin.
Tropical cyclones in this area were monitored by four Tropical Cyclone Warning Centres.
Document 5: Tropical Storm Felice was a modest tropical cyclone that lightly impacted parts of the Gulf Coast of the United States in mid-September 1970.
The effects from Felice were generally light.
Document 6: An Atlantic hurricane or tropical storm is a tropical cyclone that forms in the Atlantic Ocean, usually in the summer or fall.
The United States National Hurricane Center monitors the basin and issues reports, watches, and warnings about tropical weather systems.
Document 7: The 1971 Atlantic hurricane season was fairly active with several notable storms.
Hurricane Edith, the strongest of the season, was a Category 5 on the Saffir-Simpson scale.

topic.name: Public Spaces and Community Centers
topic.description: The documents discuss various public spaces and community centers across the United States, highlighting their history, purpose, and community significance.

Document 1: AT & T Plaza is a public space that hosts the Cloud Gate sculpture.
Millennium Park trails only Navy Pier as a Chicago tourist attraction.
Document 2: Wayzata Bay Center was an enclosed shopping mall in Wayzata, Minnesota.
The mall was shut and torn down to make way for a mixed-use retail and residential community center.
Document 3: The Oregon Civic Justice Center is a three-story former library building on the campus of Willamette University.
The building now houses several programs of Willamette University College of Law.
Document 4: Keller Fountain Park is a city park in downtown Portland, Oregon.
The central feature of the park is the concrete water fountain.
Document 5: Snyder is a hamlet within the town of Amherst in Erie County, New York, United States.
The hamlet has several commercial districts, including a modest business district along Main Street.
Document 6: The Church Administration Building serves as the headquarters of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.
The CAB has been used for meetings between church leaders and political and community leaders.
Document 7: The Suquamish Museum preserves and displays relics and records related to the Suquamish Tribe.
The new facility opened in 2012 and is triple the size of the original building.
